title: PDF Page 1
content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 1 – Float Installation 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
Product being changed: Airplane Model Cessna 182 is manufactured with a tricycle landing 
gear configuration. The airplane was originally certified to CAR 3 Amendment 3-1 through  
3-8. CFR Part 23 is proposed as the basis. 
 
CHANGE 1 – Float Installation 
 
Description of the change: 
 
 An applicant proposes to offer a float installation for the 
Cessna 182, by removing the tricycle landing gear and 
installing twin hull floats. 
Reason for change:  
 
To market a float configuration for the aircraft type. 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
 
• Replacement of the tricycle landing gear with 
twin hull floats 
• Complete removal of the forward nose gear 
• Modification of aft gear attach points to 
become the forward attach fitting for the 
floats
 
• Addition of aft float support attachment to the 
aircraft 
• Addition of fuselage shear braces 
• Addition of float rudder control and retraction 
system
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
List any Concurrent related changes.  
Nil 
List any Previous relevant changes.  
Nil

title: PDF Page 3
content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 1 – Float Installation 
 
WORKSHEET 
STEP 4 – SIGNIFICANT or NOT? 
 
 The design change is an example on the AC table? 
 The design change is close to an example on the AC table?  
 The design change is NOT on the AC table, review against the following criteria. 
 
GENERAL CONFIGURATION - Is there a change to the general configuration? 
 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change to the general configuration at the product level that is likely to require a new 
model designation because of the need to distinguish the different product with other 
product models (e.g. performance, interchangeability of major components). 
 
PRINCIPLES OF CONSTRUCTION - Is there a change to the principles of construction? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change at the product level to the materials and/or construction methods that affects 
the overall product’s operating characteristics or inherent strength and would require 
extensive re-investigation to show compliance. Examples of this are a metal to 
composite structure, a piston-propeller to turbine-propeller, or single axle main gear to 
double axle main gear. 
 
ASSUMPTIONS - Have the assumptions used for certification been invalidated? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
Changes to product level assumptions, either design or engineering, associated with 
product development, compliance demonstration, performance or operating envelope 
that by themselves are so different, that the original assumptions are invalidated and 
the existing substantiation cannot be extrapolated to cover the changed product. 
 
(1) Design assumptions are made during the development of the type certified 
product and include such criteria as passenger capacity, maximum operating 
altitude, aircraft configuration, flight loads, expected life limits and environmental 
operating range, such as flight into know icing, HIRF, etc. 
(2) Engineering assumptions  include such things as wing loading, structural load 
paths, structural strength and stiffness, basic type of propulsion method, and the 
principles or philosophy of the operat ion of systems. Changes in these 
assumptions require an examination of whether the extent of the change amounts 
to an invalidation of the assumptions includ ed for certification. Where the physics, 
kinematics, or other scientific principles involved in the change are different from 
those applicable to the original product, the assumptions may not remain valid if 
the change cannot be validly analyzed by  extrapolating the analysis from the 
original product. 
Examples may include: 
(a)  Change of an aircraft from an unpr essurized to pressurized fuselage, 
(b)  Change of operation of a fixed wing aircraft from land based to water based, and 
(c)  Operation envelope expansions that ar e outside the existing design parameters 
and capabilities. 
 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally 
designed is generally not a significant change.  In this case, the assumptions used for 
certification of the basic product remain valid and the results can be applied to cover 
the changed product with predictable effects or can be demonstrated without significant 
physical changes to the product.    
Calibrate your preliminary decision of Significant or Not Significant using similar examples in the AC tables. 
Conclusion:

content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 2 – Engine Change 
1 of 24 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
Product being changed:  DHC-3 (Otter).  The airplane was originally certified to CAR 3 
Amendment 3-1 to 3-8. CFR Part 23 is proposed as the basis. 
 
CHANGE 2 – Engine Change 
 
Description of the change: 
 
Replacement of the existing engine from a 9-cylinder radial to 
a V-8 turbocharged of the same horsepower 
Reason for change:  
 
To modernize aircraft by taking advantage of state-of –
the art powerplant 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• Change in engine mount 
• Change in engine cowling 
• Change in exhaust system 
• Change in lubrication system 
• Change in fuel grade 
• Change in cooling method (from air cooled to 
liquid cooling) 
• Change in induction system 
• Change in fire x and detector 
• Change in cockpit instrumentation 
• Re-routing of fuel lines 
• Penetrations of firewall 
• Re-routing of electrical cables 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
List any Concurrent related changes.  
Nil 
List any Previous relevant changes.  
Nil

Examples may include:

capabilities. 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally

content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 3 – Auxiliary Fuel Tank 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
Product being changed: Transport Category CRJ aircraft 
 
CASE 3 - Installation of auxiliary fuel tank 
Description of the 
change: 
Installation of a forward fuel tank capable of providing the aircraft with an 
additional 1350lbs of useable fuel (see photos and further details below). 
• The tank will be located outside the fuselage and forward of the center 
wing.  
• The forward fuel tank can be detached from the aircraft as a complete 
unit. 
•  Access panels are located at the top tank surface to avoid foreign 
object impact.  
 
• Wing /fuselage faring is attached to the tank and the fuselage. 
• Forward tank is connected to and functions as part of the center tank. 
Its operation is transparent to the pilots. 
• Forward tank finishes before center tank transfers to wings. 
Reason for change:  
 
Increased range of the aircraft for specific purposes and missions. 
 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• Forward fuel tank capable of providing the 
aircraft with an additional 1350lbs of useable 
fuel (gross weight increase). 
• Interface between existing fuel system 
and new one. 
 
• Tank is attached to fuselage at location 7.  
• 3 attachment points on each side and one at 
centerline. 
• Aft supports linked with tie rods for fuselage 
expansion under cabin pressure. 
• External machined fittings with internal 
support. 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
Nil List any Concurrent related changes. 
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Nil 
 
 
 - 1

Workshop Tab 2 Change 4 – Gravel Runway Operation 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
Product being changed: FAR 25 turboprop airplane 
 
CASE 4 - Gravel Runway Operation 
Description of the change: 
 
Installation of provisions for gravel runway operations. 
 
Operator requirement to operate off a variety of gravel runways. Reason for change:  
 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• Installation of fuselage underside protection 
material 
• Addition of low pressure tires 
• Nose wheel gravel guard 
• AFM change  
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
List any Concurrent related changes. Nil

Workshop Tab 2 Change 5 – Interior Rearrangement 
 1  
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
Product being changed: FAR 25 jet at amendment 64 
 
CASE 5 - Cabin Interior 
Description of the change: 
 
Converting a regional jet to a corporate jet - interior completion 
 
Reason for change:  
 
Customize a 50 pax to include club seating 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• Block LHS over wing emergency exit 
• Move aft non-structural bulkhead 
• Install aft lavatory and shower 
• Install forward galley 
• Replace seating with installation of 8 club seats 
• Side facing divan in aft cabin LHS  
• Relocating pass service units e.g. O2 
• Re-routing ducts, wires cables 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply)
 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
List any Concurrent related changes. Nil 
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Remove 2 forward windows LHS and RHS

source_url: C:\Users\spars\OneDrive\Desktop\FAA\OneDrive_2026-03-20\TC Aircraft Certification Speciality Course 2011\MAY 2011 Binder 3- Changed Product Rules\Workshop\WS-CPR-TAB_2_WORKSHOP_FEB_2010.pdf
page_url: C:\Users\spars\OneDrive\Desktop\FAA\OneDrive_2026-03-20\TC Aircraft Certification Speciality Course 2011\MAY 2011 Binder 3- Changed Product Rules\Workshop\WS-CPR-TAB_2_WORKSHOP_FEB_2010.pdf#page=16
title: PDF Page 16
content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 5 – Interior Rearrangement 
 
WORKSHEET – STEP 4 
SIGNIFICANT or NOT? 
 
 The design change is an example on the AC table? 
 The design change is close to an example on the AC table?  
 The design change is NOT on the AC table, review against the following criteria. 
 
GENERAL CONFIGURATION - Is there a change to the general configuration? 
 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change to the general configuration at the product level that is likely to require a new 
model designation because of the need to distinguish the different product with other 
product models (e.g. performance, interchangeability of major components). 
 
PRINCIPLES OF CONSTRUCTION - Is there a change to the principles of construction? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change at the product level to the materials and/or construction methods that affects 
the overall product’s operating characteristics or inherent strength and would require 
extensive re-investigation to show compliance. Examples of this are a metal to 
composite structure, a piston-propeller to turbine-propeller, or single axle main gear to 
double axle main gear. 
 
ASSUMPTIONS - Have the assumptions used for certification been invalidated? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
Changes to product level assumptions, either design or engineering, associated with 
product development, compliance demonstration, performance or operating envelope 
that by themselves are so different, that the original assumptions are invalidated and 
the existing substantiation cannot be extrapolated to cover the changed product. 
 
(1) Design assumptions are made during the development of the type certified 
product and include such criteria as passenger capacity, maximum operating 
altitude, aircraft configuration, flight loads, expected life limits and environmental 
operating range, such as flight into know icing, HIRF, etc. 
(2) Engineering assumptions  include such things as wing loading, structural load 
paths, structural strength and stiffness, basic type of propulsion method, and the 
principles or philosophy of the operat ion of systems. Changes in these 
assumptions require an examination of whether the extent of the change amounts 
to an invalidation of the assumptions includ ed for certification. Where the physics, 
kinematics, or other scientific principles involved in the change are different from 
those applicable to the original product, the assumptions may not remain valid if 
the change cannot be validly analyzed by  extrapolating the analysis from the 
original product. 
Examples may include: 
(a)  Change of an aircraft from an unpr essurized to pressurized fuselage, 
(b)  Change of operation of a fixed wing aircraft from land based to water based, and 
(c)  Operation envelope expansions that ar e outside the existing design parameters 
and capabilities. 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally 
designed is generally not a significant change.  In this case, the assumptions used for 
certification of the basic product remain valid and the results can be applied to cover 
the changed product with predictable effects or can be demonstrated without significant 
physical changes to the product.   
 
 
Calibrate your preliminary decision of Significant or Not Significant using similar examples in the AC tables. 
Conclusion: 
 3

content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 6: Propeller Change 
 1  
 
 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE 
 
Product being changed: DHC-8-100 (FAR 25 turbo propeller aircraft) 
 
CASE 6 – Change from a 4-bladed to 6-bladed propeller 
Description of the change: 
 
Change from a 4-bladed propeller to a 6-bladed propeller  
Reason for change:  
 
To improve performance and reduce noise 
 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• New 6-bladed propeller 
• New propeller control unit (PCU) 
• New propeller de-ice system  
• Revised engine isolators 
• Revised fairings/nacelle 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
 
List any Concurrent related changes. Nil 
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Nil

content:
Workshop Tab 2 Change 8 –Composite Wing Skins 
 1  
WORKSHEET- 
STEP 1– DEFINING THE CHANGE ; STEP 5 -AFFECTED AREAS 
 
Product being changed: Part 23 Normal Category, twin-engine turbo prop (Beech 200) 
 
CASE 8 - COMPOSITE WING SKINS (Outer Wing Panel) 
Description of the change: 
 
Replacement of outer wing panel (outboard of nacelle) aluminum 
skins and stringers with fibre composite, integrally molded, 
skin/stringer assemblies.  (Note: this is a similar change to one used 
in Design Change Approval Module of Aircraft Certification Specialty 
Course). 
Reason for change:  
 
Better surface finish, fewer parts count, lower maintenance, reduced 
manufacturing costs. 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
• New wing skin/stringer assemblies for outer 
wing (top and bottom) 
• New skin/stringer assemblies fastened with 
standard mechanical fasteners  
• Spar and ribs unchanged 
• Fuel tank capacity and plumbing unchanged 
• Aileron attachment unchanged  
• Re-routed electrical circuits (e.g. nav light 
circuit) 
• Re-routed control cable circuits (e.g. 
pulley bracket relocation) 
 
 
 
List any Concurrent related changes. Nil 
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Nil 
 
 
STEP 1 & 5 - AFFECTED AREAS (should consider all previous relevant changes) 
 
Affected Area Effects of design details  
Subpart B – Flight • Negligible 
Subpart C – Structures • Structural strength and deformation 
• Fatigue evaluation 
Subpart D - Design and Construction • Flutter 
• Wing proof of strength 
• Lightning protection of structure 
Subpart E – Powerplant • Fuel system components 
Subpart F – Equipment • Compass swing 
Subpart G – Operating Information • ICAs
